Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking 3rd blue moved player left, expanded middle white to 0-19, removed bottom white. Player position controls middle/bottom white (inverse relationship). Top white at 0-51 controlled by top blue.
- Plan: Try clicking top blue target on right side (col 3) to shrink top white toward gap at 28-29.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 18 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I understand: Level 1 was steps 0-14, Level 2 started at step 15. In Level 1, the white areas were right-aligned (cols 20-63, etc.), and the player moved left/right to expand/shrink them. When the white area reached the gap, the level completed.
In Level 2, the white areas are left-aligned (cols 0-X). The gap is at cols 28-29. The white areas can have different widths, and they seem to be controlled by the blue targets.
Let me reconsider the goal. In Level 1, the goal was to have the white area "connect" with the gap. The white area was right-aligned, so it needed to extend leftward to reach the gap.
In Level 2, the white areas are left-aligned. Maybe the goal is to have the white areas extend rightward to reach the gap? But the white areas are at cols 0-X, so they need to have width 28 (ending at col 27) to reach the gap.
Looking at the possible widths:
- Top white: 51 or 55 (both extend beyond the gap)
- Middle white: 3, 7, 11, 15, 19 (none reach the gap)
- Bottom white: 3, 7, 11 (none reach the gap)
So the middle and bottom white areas can never reach the gap with their current width options. Maybe the goal is different.
Wait, let me reconsider. In Level 1, the white area was right-aligned (cols X-63), and the gap was at cols 28-29. The goal was to have the white area extend leftward to col 27, so it "connects" with the gap.
In Level 2, the white areas are left-aligned (cols 0-X). Maybe the goal is to have the white areas extend rightward to col 27, so they "connect" with the gap from the left side.
But the middle white can only have widths 3, 7, 11, 15, 19. The maximum is 19, which doesn't reach col 27.
Unless... the white areas can have other widths that I haven't seen yet. Let me check if there are other possible widths.
Actually, looking at the history more carefully, I see that the middle white has widths 3, 7, 11, 15, 19. These are all odd numbers, and they increment by 4. The next width would be 23, then 27. If the middle white can have width 27, it would end at col 26, which is just before the gap at col 28-29.
But wait, the gap is at cols 28-29, so the white area would need to end at col 27 to "connect" with the gap. If the middle white has width 28, it would end at col 27.
Let me check if the middle white can have width 28. Looking at the pattern 3, 7, 11, 15, 19, the next values would be 23, 27, 31, etc. So 28 is not in the sequence. But 27 is, and a width of 27 would end at col 26, which is one column before the gap.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me reconsider the puzzle mechanics.
In Level 1, the player moved left/right to expand/shrink the white area. The white area was right-aligned, and the gap was at cols 28-29. When the white area extended to col 27, it "connected" with the gap, and the level completed.
In Level 2, there are multiple white areas, each controlled by a different blue target. The white areas are left-aligned. The gap is at cols 28-29.
Maybe the goal is to have all white areas have the same width, and that width matches the gap column? Or maybe the goal is to have the white areas "stack" vertically, forming a continuous column that reaches the gap?
Actually, let me look at the initial state of Level 2 (Step 15):
- Top white: cols 0-51
- Middle white: cols 0-11
- Bottom white: cols 0-7
The top white is much wider than the middle and bottom white areas. Maybe the goal is to make all white areas have the same width?
Looking at the history, I see that the top white can be 51 or 55, the middle white can be 3, 7, 11, 15, 19, and the bottom white can be 3, 7, 11. These don't overlap much, so it's unlikely that the goal is to make all white areas have the same width.
Let me think about this differently. In Level 1, the white area was right-aligned, and the player moved left/right to expand/shrink it. The gap was at cols 28-29, and the level completed when the white area reached the gap.
In Level 2, the white areas are left-aligned. Maybe the goal is similar: have the white areas reach the gap. But the white areas are at cols 0-X, so they need to extend to col 27 to reach the gap.
The top white is at cols 0-51 or 0-55, which extends beyond the gap. So the top white already "reaches" the gap.
The middle white is at cols 0-3, 0-7, 0-11, 0-15, or 0-19. None of these reach the gap.
The bottom white is at cols 0-3, 0-7, or 0-11. None of these reach the gap.
So the middle and bottom white areas need to be expanded to reach the gap. But their maximum widths are 19 and 11, respectively, which don't reach the gap.
Unless... the white areas can have other widths that I haven't seen yet. Let me try clicking the 2nd blue target multiple times to see if the middle white can expand beyond 19.
Actually, I just realized something. The middle white has widths 3, 7, 11, 15, 19. These are all of the form 4k - 1, where k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. The next value would be 4*6 - 1 = 23, then 27, 31, etc.
If the middle white can have width 27, it would end at col 26, which is just before the gap at col 28-29. But 27 is not 28, so it wouldn't "connect" with the gap.
Actually, wait. The gap is at cols 28-29. If the white area ends at col 27, it's adjacent to the gap (col 28). So the white area would "connect" with the gap.
But the middle white has maximum width 19, which ends at col 18. That's far from the gap.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is not to have the white areas reach the gap. Maybe the goal is something else.
Let me look at the Level 1 solution again. In Level 1, the player moved left/right to expand/shrink the white area. The white area was right-aligned (cols X-63), and the gap was at cols 28-29. When the white area extended to col 27, it "connected" with the gap, and the level completed.
Wait, actually, I need to check what the Level 1 solution looked like. Let me look at the history for Level 1.
Looking at the history:
- Step 0: W areas cols [(32, 63), (52, 63)]
- Step 3: W areas cols [(28, 63), (56, 63)]
- Step 4: W areas cols [(24, 63), (60, 63)]
- Step 5: W areas cols [(20, 63)]
- Step 14: W areas cols [(40, 63), (44, 63)]
- Step 15: Level 2 starts
So in Level 1, the white areas were right-aligned (cols X-63). The gap was at cols 28-29. The white areas had widths that varied, and they were controlled by the player's position.
At Step 5, there was only one white area at cols 20-63. This extends beyond the gap (cols 28-29), so it "connects" with the gap. But the level didn't complete at Step 5.
At Step 14, the white areas were at cols 40-63 and 44-63. These don't reach the gap.
At Step 15, Level 2 started. So the Level 1 solution was not at Step 5 or Step 14.
Actually, I think Level 1 completed at some point between Step 14 and Step 15. The transition from Level 1 to Level 2 happened at Step 15.
Let me check what happened at Step 14-15. The white areas changed from [(40, 63), (44, 63)] to [(0, 51), (0, 11), (0, 7)]. This is a significant change, and it's probably the Level 1 completion.
So in Level 1, the goal was to have the white areas at cols 40-63 and 44-63. But why? The gap is at cols 28-29, and the white areas at cols 40-63 and 44-63 don't reach the gap.
Actually, maybe I'm misunderstanding the Level 1 goal. Let me reconsider.
In Level 1, the white areas were right-aligned. The gap was at cols 28-29. The white areas had widths that varied based on the player's position.
Looking at the history:
- Step 0: W areas cols [(32, 63), (52, 63)] - two white areas
- Step 5: W areas cols [(20, 63)] - one white area
- Step 14: W areas cols [(40, 63), (44, 63)] - two white areas
At Step 5, there was only one white area. At Step 14, there were two white areas. The Level 1 completion happened at Step 14-15, when the white areas were at cols 40-63 and 44-63.
But why would cols 40-63 and 44-63 be the solution? The gap is at cols 28-29, and these white areas don't reach the gap.
Unless... the goal in Level 1 was not to have the white areas reach the gap. Maybe the goal was to have the white areas match a specific pattern.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the entire puzzle. Let me look at the initial state of Level 1 (Step 0):
- W areas cols [(32, 63), (52, 63)]
And the final state of Level 1 (Step 14):
- W areas cols [(40, 63), (44, 63)]
The white areas changed from [(32, 63), (52, 63)] to [(40, 63), (44, 63)]. The first white area moved right (from 32 to 40), and the second white area moved left (from 52 to 44). They met in the middle, at cols 40-63 and 44-63.
So the goal in Level 1 was to have the two white areas "meet" in the middle, forming a continuous block from col 40 to 63. The gap at cols 28-29 is not directly related to the white areas.
Wait, but the gap is at cols 28-29, and the white areas are at cols 40-63 and 44-63. They don't connect with the gap.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the gap is not at cols 28-29 in Level 1. Let me check the initial state of Level 1.
Actually, I don't have access to the initial state of Level 1. I only have the history from Step 0 onwards, which is already in Level 1.
Let me focus on Level 2. The initial state of Level 2 (Step 15) was:
- W areas cols [(0, 51), (0, 11), (0, 7)]
The gap is at cols 28-29. The white areas are left-aligned.
Looking at the history, I see that the white areas can have different widths:
- Top white: 51 or 55
- Middle white: 3, 7, 11, 15, 19
- Bottom white: 3, 7, 11
The sum of middle + bottom white widths is 18 when both exist.
Maybe the goal is to have all three white areas have the same width? But the top white has widths 51 or 55, which are much larger than the middle and bottom white widths.
Or maybe the goal is to have the white areas form a specific pattern, like a pyramid or a staircase?
Actually, let me look at the current state:
- Top white: cols 0-55
- Middle white: cols 0-15
- Bottom white: gone
The top white is at cols 0-55, which extends beyond the gap. The middle white is at cols 0-15, which doesn't reach the gap. The bottom white is gone.
Maybe the goal is to have the white areas form a "staircase" pattern, where each white area is narrower than the one above it? Or wider?
Looking at the initial state of Level 2:
- Top white: cols 0-51
- Middle white: cols 0-11
- Bottom white: cols 0-7
This is a staircase pattern, where each white area is narrower than the one above it (51 > 11 > 7).
Maybe the goal is to reverse this pattern, so that each white area is wider than the one above it? Or to have all white areas have the same width?
Actually, I think I need to stop speculating and try a different approach. Let me look at the blue targets and see if there's a pattern in how they control the white areas.
The blue targets are at:
- Top blue: rows 16-19, cols 0-3
- 2nd blue: rows 24-27, cols 0-3
- 3rd blue: rows 36-39, cols 0-3
- Bottom blue: rows 44-47, cols 0-3
Each blue target is a 4x4 block. Clicking on different parts of the blue target might control the white area differently.
In Level 1, clicking on the blue target moved the player left/right, which expanded/shrunk the white area. The blue target was a 2x4 block, and clicking on the left side moved the player left, while clicking on the right side moved the player right.
In Level 2, maybe the blue targets work similarly. Clicking on the left side of a blue target might expand the corresponding white area, while clicking on the right side might shrink it.
Let me try clicking on the left side of the top blue target to see if it shrinks the top white area.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the top blue target at col 2 (left side) and col 3 (right side). Clicking at col 2 expanded the top white from 0-51 to 0-55, and clicking at col 3 also expanded it from 0-51 to 0-55.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Looking at the history:
- Step 44: W areas cols [(0, 51), (0, 19)]
- Step 45: W areas cols [(0, 55), (0, 15)] - after clicking top blue at col 2
- Step 46: W areas cols [(0, 51), (0, 19)] - after clicking 2nd blue at col 2
- Step 47: W areas cols [(0, 51), (0, 15), (0, 3)] - after clicking bottom blue at col 2
- Step 48: W areas cols [(0, 51), (0, 19)] - after clicking 3rd blue at col 2
- Step 49: W areas cols [(0, 55), (0, 15)] - after clicking top blue at col 3
So clicking the top blue at col 2 or col 3 both expand the top white from 0-51 to 0-55. But they also affect the middle white: from 0-19 to 0-15.
This suggests that the top blue target controls both the top and middle white areas. Clicking it expands the top white and shrinks the middle white.
Similarly, the 2nd blue target controls the top and middle white areas in the opposite direction: shrinks the top white and expands the middle white.
The bottom blue target controls the bottom white area and the player's position.
The 3rd blue target controls the middle and bottom white areas.
This is getting complicated. Let me try to find a pattern.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the blue targets control the "distribution" of white area width among the three white areas. The total width is constant, and the blue targets redistribute the width among the three areas.
Looking at the history:
- Step 15: [(0, 51), (0, 11), (0, 7)] - total = 51 + 11 + 7 = 69
- Step 16: [(0, 55), (0, 7), (0, 7)] - total = 55 + 7 + 7 = 69
- Step 17: [(0, 55), (0, 3), (0, 11)] - total = 55 + 3 + 11 = 69
- Step 27: [(0, 51), (0, 3), (0, 15)] - total = 51 + 3 + 15 = 69
- Step 28: [(0, 51), (0, 7), (0, 11)] - total = 51 + 7 + 11 = 69
- Step 31: [(0, 51), (0, 19)] - total = 51 + 19 = 70 (bottom white gone)
- Step 45: [(0, 55), (0, 15)] - total = 55 + 15 = 70 (bottom white gone)
- Step 49: [(0, 55), (0, 15)] - total = 55 + 15 = 70 (bottom white gone)
So the total width is 69 when all three white areas exist, and 70 when only two white areas exist.
The total width is almost constant (69 or 70). This suggests that the blue targets redistribute the width among the white areas.
The gap is at cols 28-29. If the goal is to have all white areas end at col 27 (just before the gap), then each white area would need to have width 28. But the total width is 69 or 70, which is not 28 * 3 = 84.
So the goal is not to have all white areas end at col 27.
Maybe the goal is to have the white areas form a specific pattern, like a pyramid or a staircase?
Looking at the initial state of Level 2:
- Top white: cols 0-51
- Middle white: cols 0-11
- Bottom white: cols 0-7
This is a staircase pattern, where each white area is narrower than the one above it (51 > 11 > 7).
Maybe the goal is to reverse this pattern, so that each white area is wider than the one above it? Like 7 < 11 < 51?
But the total width is 69, so the widths would need to sum to 69. If the pattern is 7 < 11 < 51, the sum is 69. But this is the same as the initial state, just reversed.
Actually, the initial state is 51 > 11 > 7, which is already a staircase pattern. Maybe the goal is to have the white areas form a different pattern, like all equal widths?
If all three white areas have the same width, the width would be 69 / 3 = 23. But the possible widths are 51, 55 for the top white, and 3, 7, 11, 15, 19 for the middle white, and 3, 7, 11 for the bottom white. None of these is 23.
So the goal is not to have all white areas have the same width.
Let me think about this differently. In Level 1, the goal was to have the two white areas "meet" in the middle, forming a continuous block. In Level 2, maybe the goal is to have the three white areas form a continuous block?
But the white areas are at different rows, so they can't form a continuous block unless they have the same width.
Actually, wait. The white areas are at different rows:
- Top white: rows 1-19
- Middle white: rows 24-39
- Bottom white: rows 48-63
They are separated by black bars at rows 20-23 and 40-47. So they can't form a continuous block.
But the gap is at rows 40-43, cols 28-29. This is in the black bar between the middle and bottom white areas.
Maybe the goal is to have the white areas "connect" with the gap, i.e., have the white areas extend to col 27 so they are adjacent to the gap?
The top white is at cols 0-51 or 0-55, which extends beyond the gap. So the top white already "connects" with the gap.
The middle white is at cols 0-3, 0-7, 0-11, 0-15, or 0-19. None of these reach the gap.
The bottom white is at cols 0-3, 0-7, or 0-11. None of these reach the gap.
So the middle and bottom white areas need to be expanded to reach the gap. But their maximum widths are 19 and 11, respectively, which don't reach the gap.
Unless... the white areas can have other widths that I haven't seen yet. Let me try clicking the 2nd blue target multiple times to see if the middle white can expand beyond 19.
Actually, I just realized that I've been clicking the blue targets at col 2, which is on the left side. Maybe clicking at col 0 or col 1 would have a different effect.
Let me try clicking the 2nd blue target at col 0 to see if it expands the middle white beyond 19.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Total white width is ~69-70, redistributed among 3 areas by blue targets. Top white 51/55, middle 3/7/11/15/19, bottom 3/7/11. Need to find if widths can go higher.
Plan: Try clicking 2nd blue at col 0 to see if middle white can expand beyond 19 toward gap at 28-29.
